text:
The myth that won't die in PLG circles:

"If activation is strong, expansion will follow."

Most product teams build their entire 2024 roadmap around this. Optimize time-to-value, nail the aha moment, watch ARR compound.

Then they look at net revenue retention 18 months later and wonder what went wrong.

Here's what falls apart when you actually talk to buyers:

The person who activates is almost never the person who expands the contract. Activators are individual users solving a personal workflow. Expanders are managers solving a team or org problem. Two different buyers. Two different jobs.

Strong activation tells you the product works. It tells you nothing about whether it's becoming infrastructure.

What's actually true:

1. Activation predicts retention, not expansion
2. Expansion is driven by *cross-user dependency* — how many people rely on what one user produced
3. The metric to watch isn't DAU/WAU. It's "artifacts shared across team boundaries" per account

The shift to make: stop optimizing the single-user funnel as your growth engine. Start instrumenting collaboration depth as a leading indicator of expansion.

Where this goes in the next 12-18 months: the best PLG companies will split their PM org into an activation pod and a network-effects pod, with separate metrics and separate roadmaps. The "one funnel to rule them all" era is ending.

What's the expansion signal your team trusts most right now?

#PLG #ProductManagement #SaaS
